title: Getting started with dynR
text: Resting-state fMRI measures BOLD signal across brain regions time. Static functional connectivity (FC) compresses single correlation matrix — time-averaged co-activation pattern across entire scan. Convenient, discards everything changes. Dynamic functional connectivity (dynFC) treats time dimension seriously. asks: pattern co-activation brain regions shift scan, can shifts tell us something cognition, clinical state, neural mechanisms? dynR computes dynFC representations preprocessed multivariate timeseries, implementing two complementary families methods: families produce outputs feed directly stateR brain state quantification.

title: Getting started with dynR
text: Choosing phase-based correlation-based methods depends research question: new dynFC, phase-based / LEiDA pipeline good starting point: requires window length decision produces compact, interpretable features. Sliding-window FC directly comparable broader published literature easier relate task structure behavioural events.

title: Getting started with dynR
text: dynR ships resting-state BOLD fMRI timeseries edge-ts repository (Faskowitz et al., 2020), parcellated 200 regions interest. dataset used throughout three vignettes. methods restricted fMRI — apply equally EEG, LFP, band-limited neurophysiological signal. 200 parcels, 600 timepoints — approximately 20 minutes TR = 2 s.
code: data(ts)   # [200 × 600] BOLD timeseries data(fc)   # [200 × 200] static FC matrix (ground truth)  dim(ts) #> [1] 200 600 dim(fc) #> [1] 200 200

title: Getting started with dynR
code: BOLD timeseries [N × Tmax]         │         ├─── bandpass_filter()       optional: restrict to 0.01–0.1 Hz         │         ├─── PHASE-BASED ─────────────────────────────────────────────         │     hilbert_phases()   →  instantaneous phases  [N × Tmax]         │     dyn_phase_lock()   →  dPL matrices + LEiDA eigenvectors         │     kuramoto()         →  synchrony, metastability, entropy         │         └─── CORRELATION-BASED ───────────────────────────────────────               corr_slide()      →  FC matrices  [N × N × windows]               cofluct()         →  edge time series + RSS               corr_corr()       →  FC recurrence  [Tmax × Tmax]                     │                     ▼               stateR  ──  fractional occupancy                       ──  dwell time                       ──  Markov transitions

title: Getting started with dynR
code: # Instantaneous phases via the Hilbert transform phases <- hilbert_phases(ts)  # Dynamic phase-locking matrices + LEiDA eigenvectors dpl <- dyn_phase_lock(phases) cat("LEiDA matrix:", nrow(dpl$leida), "timepoints ×",     ncol(dpl$leida), "parcels\n") #> LEiDA matrix: 580 timepoints × 200 parcels  # Kuramoto order parameter kop <- kuramoto(phases) cat("Metastability:", round(kop$metastability, 4), "\n") #> Metastability: 0.0796 ggplot(data.frame(t = seq_along(kop$synchrony), R = kop$synchrony),        aes(x = t, y = R)) +   geom_line(colour = "#8D9FD7", linewidth = 0.7) +   geom_hline(yintercept = mean(kop$synchrony),              colour = "#9E3C30", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.9) +   scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1)) +   labs(x = "Timepoint", y = "R(t)",        title = "Kuramoto order parameter",        subtitle = paste0("Metastability = ",                          round(kop$metastability, 4))) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +   theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

title: Getting started with dynR
code: # Sliding-window FC: 30-timepoint windows, step 5 sw <- corr_slide(ts, window = 30, step = 5) cat("Windows:", dim(sw$corr_mats)[3], "\n") #> Windows: 115  # Edge-centric cofluctuations ec <- cofluct(ts) cat("Edges:", nrow(ec$edge_ts), "× Timepoints:", ncol(ec$edge_ts), "\n") #> Edges: 19900 × Timepoints: 600 ggplot(data.frame(t = seq_along(ec$rss), rss = ec$rss),        aes(x = t, y = rss)) +   geom_line(colour = "#8D9FD7", linewidth = 0.7) +   geom_hline(yintercept = mean(ec$rss) + 2 * sd(ec$rss),              colour = "#9E3C30", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.9) +   labs(x = "Timepoint", y = "RSS",        title = "Root-sum-square cofluctuation",        subtitle = "Dashed line: mean + 2 SD") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +   theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

what: Sanity check: single window equals static FC
title: Getting started with dynR
text: single window spanning full timeseries must recover static FC matrix exactly — useful first validation new dataset.
code: full_window <- corr_slide(ts, window = ncol(ts)) max_diff    <- max(abs(full_window$corr_mats[, , 1] - fc)) cat("Max deviation from static FC:", formatC(max_diff, format = "e"), "\n") #> Max deviation from static FC: 8.8818e-16

title: Getting started with dynR
text: vignette("phase-based-fc") — Hilbert transform, LEiDA, K-means, Kuramoto order parameter depth vignette("sliding-window-fc") — window length trade-offs, edge cofluctuations, FC recurrence stateR — downstream brain state metrics

title: Getting started with dynR
text: Faskowitz, J. et al. (2020). Edge-centric functional network representations human cerebral cortex reveal overlapping system-level architecture. Nature Neuroscience, 23(12), 1644–1654. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41593-020-00719-y

title: Phase-based dynamic FC: Hilbert transform, LEiDA, and Kuramoto
text: Phase-based dynFC methods characterise connectivity instantaneous phase signal. Rather asking correlated two regions window, ask: two regions synchronised exact moment — oscillating phase, anti-phase? perspective originates physics coupled oscillators. channel’s timeseries, bandpass-filtered frequency band interest, behaves like noisy oscillator. Hilbert transform extracts instantaneous phase oscillation every timepoint. Two channels phase-locked phases move together; anti-phase synchrony phases consistently offset π radians. Although vignette uses BOLD fMRI data refers parcels TR throughout, methods apply band-limited neurophysiological signal — including EEG narrow-band epochs, LFP, MEG. dynR implements three phase-based methods:

title: Phase-based dynamic FC: Hilbert transform, LEiDA, and Kuramoto
text: Phase extraction via Hilbert transform assumes signal approximately monocomponent — dominated oscillations within single frequency band. Applying bandpass filter transform therefore merely conventional; necessary. Without , broadband noise low-frequency drift corrupt phase estimate, producing meaningless phase differences parcels. resting-state fMRI TR = 2 s, standard band 0.01–0.1 Hz, preserves slow fluctuations drive functional connectivity attenuating scanner drift (< 0.01 Hz) physiological noise (> 0.1 Hz). apply() call filters parcel (row) independently; t() restores N×TmaxN × Tmax convention expected downstream functions.
code: ts_filt <- apply(ts, 1, bandpass_filter,                  flp = 0.01, fhi = 0.1, delt = 2, order = 2) ts_filt <- t(ts_filt)   # restore [N × Tmax] orientation dim(ts_filt) #> [1] 200 600

what: Step 2: Instantaneous phases via the Hilbert transform
title: Phase-based dynamic FC: Hilbert transform, LEiDA, and Kuramoto
text: hilbert_phases() constructs analytic signal parcel — complex-valued representation whose argument gives instantaneous phase angle every timepoint. row parcel; column timepoint. Values radians.
code: phases <- hilbert_phases(ts_filt) dim(phases)    # [200 × 600] #> [1] 200 600 range(phases)  # in [-pi, pi] #> [1] -3.141479  3.141498

previous_headings: Step 3: Dynamic phase-locking matrix (dPL) and LEiDA
title: Phase-based dynamic FC: Hilbert transform, LEiDA, and Kuramoto
text: timepoint t, dyn_phase_lock() constructs N × N phase-locking matrix: dPLij(t)=cos⁡(ϕi(t)−ϕj(t))\text{dPL}_{ij}(t) = \cos\!\bigl(\phi_i(t) - \phi_j(t)\bigr) Entry (, j) = 1 parcels j perfectly phase; −1 perfectly anti-phase. matrix symmetric ones diagonal. Ten timepoints trimmed end avoid Hilbert transform edge effects.

previous_headings: Step 3: Dynamic phase-locking matrix (dPL) and LEiDA
title: Phase-based dynamic FC: Hilbert transform, LEiDA, and Kuramoto
text: dominant structure dPL matrix captured leading eigenvector — eigenvector corresponding largest eigenvalue. vector one entry per parcel: Parcels sign synchronised moment. Parcels opposite signs anti-phase. magnitude entry reflects strongly parcel participates dominant pattern. tracking vector evolves time clustering , identify recurring whole-brain phase-locking patterns — brain states — without ever specifying window length. LEiDA framework (Cabral et al., 2017).
code: dpl <- dyn_phase_lock(phases) dim(dpl$sync_conn)   # [200 × 200 × 580] #> [1] 200 200 580 dim(dpl$leida)       # [580 × 200] #> [1] 580 200 t1 <- dpl$sync_conn[, , 1] cat("Symmetric:    ", isTRUE(all.equal(t1, t(t1))), "\n") #> Symmetric:     TRUE cat("Diagonal = 1: ", all(abs(diag(t1) - 1) < 1e-10), "\n") #> Diagonal = 1:  TRUE

what: Step 4: Brain state discovery with K-means
title: Phase-based dynamic FC: Hilbert transform, LEiDA, and Kuramoto
text: LEiDA vectors clustered across time identify recurring phase-locking patterns. K-means standard choice (Cabral et al., 2017; Lord et al., 2019).

previous_headings: Step 4: Brain state discovery with K-means
title: Phase-based dynamic FC: Hilbert transform, LEiDA, and Kuramoto
text: universal rule K. Typical choices resting-state research range 2–3 (coarse global organisation) 5–10 (finer network distinctions). nstart argument critical — K-means sensitive initialisation, running many random restarts keeping best solution essential.
code: set.seed(42) K  <- 5 km <- kmeans(dpl$leida, centers = K, nstart = 100, iter.max = 500) table(km$cluster) #>  #>   1   2   3   4   5  #>  65 144 112  98 161

previous_headings: Step 4: Brain state discovery with K-means
title: Phase-based dynamic FC: Hilbert transform, LEiDA, and Kuramoto
text: state sequence (km$cluster) feeds directly stateR fractional occupancy, dwell time, Markov transition analysis.
code: state_cols <- c("#8D9FD7", "#9E3C30", "#754D71", "#341C5D", "#E19A8F")  ggplot(data.frame(t     = seq_along(km$cluster),                   state = factor(km$cluster)),        aes(x = t, y = 1, fill = state)) +   geom_tile(height = 1) +   scale_fill_manual(values = state_cols, name = "State") +   labs(x = "Timepoint", y = NULL,        title = "Brain state sequence (K = 5)") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +   theme(axis.text.y  = element_blank(),         axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),         panel.grid   = element_blank())

title: Phase-based dynamic FC: Hilbert transform, LEiDA, and Kuramoto
text: Kuramoto order parameter R(t) measures degree global phase synchrony across parcels timepoint: R(t)=1N|∑j=1Neiϕj(t)|R(t) = \frac{1}{N} \left| \sum_{j=1}^{N} e^{\phi_j(t)} \right| R = 1 means parcels perfectly phase-locked. R = 0 means phases uniformly distributed — coherent synchrony anywhere.

title: Phase-based dynamic FC: Hilbert transform, LEiDA, and Kuramoto
text: standard deviation R(t) full scan metastability index: measure much global synchronisation level fluctuates. High metastability: brain moves fluidly synchronised desynchronised states — flexible, exploratory dynamic repertoire. Low metastability: synchronisation rigid, less spontaneous switching. Metastability linked cognitive flexibility (Deco et al., 2017). Reductions observed ageing (Cabral et al., 2014) certain psychiatric conditions. Psilocybin markedly increases metastability, consistent pharmacologically expanded dynamic repertoire (Lord et al., 2019).
code: kop <- kuramoto(phases, base = 2, n_bits = 8)  cat("Metastability:", round(kop$metastability, 4), "\n") #> Metastability: 0.0788 cat("Entropy (bits):", round(kop$entropy, 4), "\n") #> Entropy (bits): 6.1722 df_kop <- data.frame(t = seq_along(kop$synchrony), R = kop$synchrony)  ggplot(df_kop, aes(x = t, y = R)) +   geom_line(colour = "#8D9FD7", linewidth = 0.7) +   geom_hline(yintercept = mean(kop$synchrony),              colour = "#9E3C30", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.9) +   annotate("text",            x     = max(df_kop$t) * 0.02,            y     = mean(kop$synchrony) + 0.04,            label = paste0("Mean = ", round(mean(kop$synchrony), 3)),            colour = "#9E3C30", size = 3.5, hjust = 0) +   scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1)) +   labs(x = "Timepoint", y = "R(t)",        title = "Kuramoto order parameter",        subtitle = paste0("Metastability = ", round(kop$metastability, 4),                          "   |   Entropy = ",                          round(kop$entropy, 3), " bits")) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +   theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

title: Phase-based dynamic FC: Hilbert transform, LEiDA, and Kuramoto

title: Correlation-based dynamic FC: sliding windows and edge cofluctuations
text: Correlation-based dynFC methods characterise functional connectivity evolves time either windowing timeseries short segments computing Pearson correlations within window, analysing instantaneous co-activation parcel pairs continuous edge time series — windowing required. dynR implements three correlation-based functions: Although vignette uses BOLD fMRI data throughout, methods apply multivariate timeseries pairwise correlations carry meaningful information, including EEG LFP.

title: Correlation-based dynamic FC: sliding windows and edge cofluctuations
text: Unlike phase-based methods, sliding-window FC requires commit window length. trade-fundamental: Short windows (< 30 TRs): high temporal resolution, FC estimates noisy — reliable Pearson correlations require sufficient timepoints relative number parcels. Long windows (> 60 TRs): stable, low-noise FC estimates, rapid state transitions averaged away. Typical choice: 30–60 TRs (60–120 s TR = 2 s), step size 1 TR fine temporal resolution larger steps efficiency (Hutchison et al., 2013; Leonardi & Van De Ville, 2015). dataset (TR = 2 s, 600 timepoints), window choices look like:
code:
TRs <- c(20, 30, 45, 60) data.frame(   `Window (TRs)` = TRs,   `Duration (s)` = TRs * 2,   `N windows (step = 5)` = floor((600 - TRs) / 5) + 1L,   check.names = FALSE ) #>   Window (TRs) Duration (s) N windows (step = 5) #> 1           20           40                  117 #> 2           30           60                  115 #> 3           45           90                  112 #> 4           60          120                  109 sw <- corr_slide(ts, window = 30, step = 5) cat("Window:    30 TRs (60 s at TR = 2 s)
") #> Window:    30 TRs (60 s at TR = 2 s) cat("Step:       5 TRs (10 s)
") #> Step:       5 TRs (10 s) cat("N windows:", dim(sw$corr_mats)[3], "
") #> N windows: 115

title: Correlation-based dynamic FC: sliding windows and edge cofluctuations
text: single window spanning full timeseries must recover static FC matrix exactly.
code: full     <- corr_slide(ts, window = ncol(ts)) max_diff <- max(abs(full$corr_mats[, , 1] - fc)) cat("Max deviation from static FC:", formatC(max_diff, format = "e"), "\n") #> Max deviation from static FC: 8.8818e-16

title: Correlation-based dynamic FC: sliding windows and edge cofluctuations
text: standard deviation edge across windows — FC variability — reveals connections drive dynamic signal. Edges high variability ones genuinely fluctuate; edges near-zero variability essentially static.
code: n_parcels      <- dim(sw$corr_mats)[1] ut             <- which(upper.tri(diag(n_parcels)), arr.ind = TRUE) edge_by_window <- apply(sw$corr_mats, 3, function(m) m[ut]) edge_sd        <- apply(edge_by_window, 1, sd)  ggplot(data.frame(sd = edge_sd), aes(x = sd)) +   geom_histogram(bins = 60, fill = "#8D9FD7", colour = "#341C5D",                  linewidth = 0.3) +   geom_vline(xintercept = mean(edge_sd),              colour = "#9E3C30", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.9) +   labs(x = "SD across windows", y = "Number of edges",        title = "FC variability distribution",        subtitle = paste0("Mean edge SD = ", round(mean(edge_sd), 4))) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +   theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

title: Correlation-based dynamic FC: sliding windows and edge cofluctuations
text: edge-centric framework (Esfahlani et al., 2020; Faskowitz et al., 2020) abandons window entirely. Instead, parcel’s timeseries z-standardised element-wise product every unique parcel pair computed every timepoint: etsij(t)=zi(t)⋅zj(t)\text{ets}_{ij}(t) = z_i(t) \cdot z_j(t) result edge time series — continuous, frame--frame estimate co-activation every pair.
code: ec      <- cofluct(ts) n_edges <- nrow(ec$edge_ts) cat("Edges (N*(N-1)/2):", n_edges, "\n") #> Edges (N*(N-1)/2): 19900 cat("Timepoints:       ", ncol(ec$edge_ts), "\n") #> Timepoints:        600

title: Correlation-based dynamic FC: sliding windows and edge cofluctuations
text: RSS vector summarises total co-activation amplitude across edges timepoint: RSS(t)=∑(,j)etsij(t)2\text{RSS}(t) = \sqrt{\sum_{(,j)} \text{ets}_{ij}(t)^2} High-RSS frames moments unusually strong, synchronised co-activation across whole brain. high-amplitude events shown disproportionately drive structure static FC matrix — small fraction timepoints accounts long-run average connectivity (Esfahlani et al., 2020).
code: thr    <- mean(ec$rss) + 2 * sd(ec$rss) df_rss <- data.frame(t = seq_along(ec$rss), rss = ec$rss,                      high = ec$rss > thr)  ggplot(df_rss, aes(x = t, y = rss)) +   geom_line(colour = "#8D9FD7", linewidth = 0.6) +   geom_hline(yintercept = thr,              colour = "#9E3C30", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.9) +   annotate("text",            x = max(df_rss$t) * 0.02, y = thr + 0.8,            label = paste0("Mean + 2 SD  (n = ",                           sum(df_rss$high), " frames)"),            colour = "#9E3C30", size = 3.5, hjust = 0) +   labs(x = "Timepoint", y = "RSS",        title = "Root-sum-square cofluctuation",        subtitle = paste0(round(mean(df_rss$high) * 100, 1),                          "% of timepoints exceed threshold")) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +   theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

title: Correlation-based dynamic FC: sliding windows and edge cofluctuations
text: corr_corr() computes Tmax×TmaxTmax × Tmax matrix pairwise correlations edge time series. Entry (t1, t2) answers: similar brain’s whole-network co-activation pattern timepoint t1 t2? makes temporal recurrence matrix — fingerprint often brain revisits similar FC configurations. Key features look : Bright -diagonal blocks: brain revisiting similar co-activation states different times. Diagonal block structure: sustained states persist consecutive timepoints. Sparse, diffuse structure: rapidly shifting, non-recurrent dynamics.  Warm colours indicate timepoints brain similar connectivity configurations; cool colours indicate dissimilar states. Visible block structure along diagonal suggests sustained, recurring FC patterns.
code: cc <- corr_corr(ts) cat("Dimensions:", nrow(cc), "×", ncol(cc), "\n") #> Dimensions: 600 × 600 pal <- colorRampPalette(c("#341C5D", "#E8ECF8", "#9E3C30"))(256) par(mar = c(4, 4, 3, 2)) image(seq_len(nrow(cc)), seq_len(ncol(cc)), cc,       col  = pal,       xlab = "Timepoint", ylab = "Timepoint",       main = "FC recurrence (correlation of correlations)")

what: Brain state analysis from sliding-window FC
title: Correlation-based dynamic FC: sliding windows and edge cofluctuations
text: upper triangle window’s FC matrix can vectorised clustered K-means identify recurring connectivity states. Note dimensionality: feature vector N(N−1)/2 entries — 19,900 200 parcels. high dimensionality makes K-means convergence slower noisier LEiDA case, feature vector N entries. smaller datasets single-subject analyses, LEiDA approach (see vignette("phase-based-fc")) often preferable.  state sequence feeds directly stateR fractional occupancy, dwell time, Markov transition analysis.
code: features <- t(apply(sw$corr_mats, 3, function(m) m[ut]))  set.seed(42) K  <- 5 km <- kmeans(features, centers = K, nstart = 100, iter.max = 500) state_cols <- c("#8D9FD7", "#9E3C30", "#754D71", "#341C5D", "#E19A8F")  ggplot(data.frame(t     = seq_along(km$cluster),                   state = factor(km$cluster)),        aes(x = t, y = 1, fill = state)) +   geom_tile(height = 1) +   scale_fill_manual(values = state_cols, name = "State") +   labs(x = "Window", y = NULL,        title = "Sliding-window brain state sequence (K = 5)") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +   theme(axis.text.y  = element_blank(),         axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),         panel.grid   = element_blank())

what: dynR — Dynamic Connectivity for Neurophysiological Timeseries
title: dynR — Dynamic Connectivity for Neurophysiological Timeseries
text: R port Python dynfc library computing dynamic connectivity (dynFC) representations multivariate neurophysiological timeseries — BOLD fMRI, EEG, LFP, related signals.  ⚠️ dynR early development formally tested. API may change without notice, estimation results yet validated reference implementation, package undergone peer review. Use caution verify outputs independently using research context.

title: dynR — Dynamic Connectivity for Neurophysiological Timeseries
text: dynR computes dynFC representations preprocessed multivariate timeseries, providing upstream computation layer dynamic connectivity analysis. full R port Python dynfc library, motivated reproducibility: R + renv provides stable long-term environment Python dependency chains research pipelines. Although bundled example data several vignettes use BOLD fMRI, methods applicable band-limited neurophysiological signal phase relationships pairwise correlations carry meaningful information — including EEG, LFP, MEG. outputs dynR feed directly stateR brain state quantification (fractional occupancy, dwell time, Markov transitions).

title: dynR — Dynamic Connectivity for Neurophysiological Timeseries
code: Multivariate timeseries  [N × Tmax]         │         ▼      dynR                 ← this package   (dynFC representations)         │         ▼      stateR   (brain state metrics: FO, dwell time, Markov transitions)

title: dynR — Dynamic Connectivity for Neurophysiological Timeseries
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("circadia-bio/dynR")

title: dynR — Dynamic Connectivity for Neurophysiological Timeseries
code: library(dynR)  # Simulated timeseries: 80 channels, 300 timepoints set.seed(42) ts <- matrix(rnorm(80 * 300), nrow = 80, ncol = 300)  # 1. Bandpass filter (e.g. fMRI: TR = 2 s, 0.01–0.1 Hz) ts_filt <- apply(ts, 1, bandpass_filter, flp = 0.01, fhi = 0.1, delt = 2) ts_filt <- t(ts_filt)  # 2. Phase-based: LEiDA + Kuramoto phases <- hilbert_phases(ts_filt) dpl    <- dyn_phase_lock(phases)   # dpl$leida: [280 × 80] LEiDA eigenvectors kop    <- kuramoto(phases)         # kop$metastability, kop$entropy  # 3. Cluster LEiDA vectors → feed into stateR km <- kmeans(dpl$leida, centers = 5, nstart = 100)  # 4. Correlation-based: sliding-window FC + cofluctuations sw <- corr_slide(ts_filt, window = 30, step = 5) ec <- cofluct(ts_filt)             # ec$edge_ts, ec$rss

title: dynR — Dynamic Connectivity for Neurophysiological Timeseries
text: timeseries inputs follow: rows = channels/parcels (N), columns = timepoints (Tmax) matches [N, Tmax] convention original Python dynfc package.

title: Butterworth bandpass filter — bandpass_filter
text: Design apply zero-phase Butterworth bandpass filter signal, using scipy-compatible steady-state initial conditions (equivalent scipy.signal.sosfiltfilt / scipy.signal.filtfilt padtype = "odd").

title: Butterworth bandpass filter — bandpass_filter
code: bandpass_filter(x, flp, fhi, delt, order = 2)

title: Butterworth bandpass filter — bandpass_filter
text: x Numeric vector. Signal filtered. flp Numeric. Low-pass cutoff frequency (Hz). fhi Numeric. High-pass cutoff frequency (Hz). delt Numeric. Sampling interval seconds (.e. TR fMRI). order Integer. Filter order. Default 2.

title: Butterworth bandpass filter — bandpass_filter
text: Numeric vector. Zero-phase filtered signal, length x.

title: Butterworth bandpass filter — bandpass_filter
text: gsignal::filtfilt() uses zero initial conditions, produces edge transients ~0.24 signal units real fMRI data. implementation replicates scipy's lfilter_zi approach: forward backward passes initialised steady state scaled first sample pass, reducing edge error machine precision.

title: Butterworth bandpass filter — bandpass_filter
code: set.seed(1) x <- rnorm(200) x_filt <- bandpass_filter(x, flp = 0.01, fhi = 0.1, delt = 2, order = 2)

title: Edge-centric cofluctuation analysis — cofluct
text: Compute edge time series root-sum-square (RSS) cofluctuations z-standardised BOLD signal. Implements edge-centric FC framework Esfahlani et al. (2020) Faskowitz et al. (2020).

title: Edge-centric cofluctuation analysis — cofluct
code: cofluct(timeseries, k = 1)

title: Edge-centric cofluctuation analysis — cofluct
text: timeseries Numeric matrix [N × Tmax]. BOLD signal N parcels rows Tmax timepoints columns. k Integer. Upper-triangle offset. k = 1 (default) excludes diagonal; k = 0 includes .

title: Edge-centric cofluctuation analysis — cofluct
text: list : edge_ts Numeric matrix [n_edges × Tmax]. Edge time series, one row per unique parcel pair. rss Numeric vector [Tmax]. Root-sum-square cofluctuation timepoint.

title: Edge-centric cofluctuation analysis — cofluct
text: Esfahlani, F. Z. et al. (2020). High-amplitude cofluctuations cortical activity drive functional connectivity. PNAS, 117(45), 28393–28401. doi:10.1073/pnas.2005531117 Faskowitz, J. et al. (2020). Edge-centric functional network representations human cerebral cortex reveal overlapping system-level architecture. Nature Neuroscience, 23(12), 1644–1654. doi:10.1038/s41593-020-00719-y

title: Edge-centric cofluctuation analysis — cofluct
code: set.seed(1) ts <- matrix(rnorm(10 * 200), nrow = 10, ncol = 200) res <- cofluct(ts) dim(res$edge_ts)  # n_edges x 200 #> [1]  45 200

title: Correlation of correlations matrix — corr_corr
text: Compute correlation edge time series across timepoints, producing [Tmax × Tmax] "correlation correlations" matrix (Hansen et al., 2015).

title: Correlation of correlations matrix — corr_corr
code: corr_corr(timeseries, k = 1)

title: Correlation of correlations matrix — corr_corr
text: timeseries Numeric matrix [N × Tmax]. BOLD signal. k Integer. Upper-triangle offset passed cofluct(). Default 1.

title: Correlation of correlations matrix — corr_corr
text: Numeric matrix [Tmax × Tmax].

title: Correlation of correlations matrix — corr_corr
text: Hansen, E. C. . et al. (2015). Functional connectivity dynamics: Modeling switching behavior resting state. NeuroImage, 105, 525–535. doi:10.1016/j.neuroimage.2014.11.001

title: Correlation of correlations matrix — corr_corr
code: set.seed(1) ts <- matrix(rnorm(10 * 100), nrow = 10, ncol = 100) cc <- corr_corr(ts) dim(cc)  # 100 x 100 #> [1] 100 100

title: Sliding window correlation — corr_slide
text: Compute functional connectivity matrices sliding windows BOLD timeseries (Hansen et al., 2015).

title: Sliding window correlation — corr_slide
code: corr_slide(timeseries, window, step = NULL)

title: Sliding window correlation — corr_slide
text: timeseries Numeric matrix [N × Tmax]. BOLD signal N parcels rows Tmax timepoints columns. window Integer. Window size timepoints. step Integer. Step window onsets. Defaults window (non-overlapping windows).

title: Sliding window correlation — corr_slide
text: list : corr_mats Array [N, N, n_windows] Pearson correlation matrices. idx Integer vector 1-indexed window onset positions.

title: Sliding window correlation — corr_slide
text: Hansen, E. C. . et al. (2015). Functional connectivity dynamics: Modeling switching behavior resting state. NeuroImage, 105, 525–535. doi:10.1016/j.neuroimage.2014.11.001

title: Sliding window correlation — corr_slide
code: set.seed(1) ts <- matrix(rnorm(10 * 200), nrow = 10, ncol = 200) res <- corr_slide(ts, window = 20) dim(res$corr_mats)  # 10 x 10 x 10 #> [1] 10 10 10

what: Euclidean distance between consecutive points — do_euclid
title: Euclidean distance between consecutive points — do_euclid
text: Compute Euclidean distance consecutive pair rows matrix — typically trajectory PC space.

title: Euclidean distance between consecutive points — do_euclid
code: do_euclid(x)

title: Euclidean distance between consecutive points — do_euclid
text: x Numeric matrix [n_points × n_dims].

title: Euclidean distance between consecutive points — do_euclid
text: Numeric vector length nrow(x). first element always 0 (previous point).

title: Euclidean distance between consecutive points — do_euclid
code: set.seed(1) pcs <- matrix(rnorm(50 * 3), nrow = 50, ncol = 3) d <- do_euclid(pcs) length(d)  # 50 #> [1] 50

title: Dynamic phase-locking matrix (dPL) — dyn_phase_lock
text: Compute instantaneous phase-locking matrices parcel-level phase time series extract leading eigenvectors via get_leida(). first last 10 timepoints discarded avoid edge effects Hilbert transform.

title: Dynamic phase-locking matrix (dPL) — dyn_phase_lock
code: dyn_phase_lock(phases)

title: Dynamic phase-locking matrix (dPL) — dyn_phase_lock
text: phases Numeric matrix [N × Tmax]. Instantaneous phases radians, returned hilbert_phases().

title: Dynamic phase-locking matrix (dPL) — dyn_phase_lock
text: list : sync_conn Array [N, N, Tmax-20]. Instantaneous phase-locking matrices, one per (trimmed) timepoint. leida Matrix [Tmax-20, N]. Leading eigenvectors get_leida().

title: Dynamic phase-locking matrix (dPL) — dyn_phase_lock
text: Cabral, J. et al. (2017). Cognitive performance healthy older adults relates spontaneous switching states functional connectivity rest. Scientific Reports, 7(1), 5135. doi:10.1038/s41598-017-05425-7 Lord, L.-D. et al. (2019). Dynamical exploration repertoire brain networks rest modulated psilocybin. NeuroImage, 199, 127–142. doi:10.1016/j.neuroimage.2019.05.060

title: Dynamic phase-locking matrix (dPL) — dyn_phase_lock
code: set.seed(1) ts <- matrix(rnorm(10 * 200), nrow = 10, ncol = 200) phases <- hilbert_phases(ts) res <- dyn_phase_lock(phases) dim(res$sync_conn)  # 10 x 10 x 180 #> [1]  10  10 180 dim(res$leida)      # 180 x 10 #> [1] 180  10

title: State transition probabilities (Markov analysis) — dyn_transitions
text: long-format data frame cluster labels ordered time, compute first-order Markov transition probabilities brain states: source state, fraction transitions lead target state.

title: State transition probabilities (Markov analysis) — dyn_transitions
code: dyn_transitions(tbl, vars, cVar, sortBy, groupBy, remIntra = FALSE)

title: State transition probabilities (Markov analysis) — dyn_transitions
text: tbl data frame long format (one row per timepoint). vars Character vector grouping / covariate column names preserve output (e.g. c("sub", "ses", "age", "sex")). cVar Character. Name column holding integer cluster labels. sortBy Character vector column names used sort rows within group transitions computed (typically c("sub", "ses", "ttime")). groupBy Character vector column names define independent sequences (typically c("sub", "ses")). Transitions never computed across group boundaries. remIntra Logical. TRUE, self-transitions (state → state) removed normalising probabilities. Default FALSE.

title: State transition probabilities (Markov analysis) — dyn_transitions
text: nested tibble columns tag, source, target, data. tag encodes transition "<source>_<target>". data element per-group tibble columns inherited vars plus: n Raw transition count. tot Total transitions source group. nCount Transition probability (n / tot).

title: State transition probabilities (Markov analysis) — dyn_transitions
text: Ported clusters_markov() neonatal_dfc analysis pipeline (França et al., Nat Commun).

title: State transition probabilities (Markov analysis) — dyn_transitions
code: set.seed(1) df <- data.frame(   sub   = rep(c("A", "B"), each = 50),   ses   = 1L,   ttime = rep(seq_len(50), 2),   clus4 = sample(1:4, 100, replace = TRUE) ) tr <- dyn_transitions(   df,   vars    = c("sub", "ses"),   cVar    = "clus4",   sortBy  = c("sub", "ses", "ttime"),   groupBy = c("sub", "ses") ) tr #> # A tibble: 16 × 4 #> # Groups:   tag, source, target [16] #>    source target tag   data             #>     <int>  <int> <chr> <list>           #>  1      1      1 1_1   <tibble [2 × 5]> #>  2      1      2 1_2   <tibble [2 × 5]> #>  3      1      3 1_3   <tibble [2 × 5]> #>  4      1      4 1_4   <tibble [2 × 5]> #>  5      2      1 2_1   <tibble [2 × 5]> #>  6      2      2 2_2   <tibble [2 × 5]> #>  7      2      3 2_3   <tibble [2 × 5]> #>  8      2      4 2_4   <tibble [2 × 5]> #>  9      3      1 3_1   <tibble [2 × 5]> #> 10      3      2 3_2   <tibble [2 × 5]> #> 11      3      3 3_3   <tibble [2 × 5]> #> 12      3      4 3_4   <tibble [2 × 5]> #> 13      4      1 4_1   <tibble [2 × 5]> #> 14      4      2 4_2   <tibble [2 × 5]> #> 15      4      3 4_3   <tibble [2 × 5]> #> 16      4      4 4_4   <tibble [2 × 5]>

title: Functional connectivity matrix (200 parcels) — fc
text: Static functional connectivity matrix derived ts computing full-timeseries Pearson correlation across 200 parcel pairs. Serves ground-truth reference validating sliding-window phase-based dynFC methods window spans full timeseries.

title: Functional connectivity matrix (200 parcels) — fc
code: fc

title: Functional connectivity matrix (200 parcels) — fc
text: numeric matrix 200 rows 200 columns. Diagonal 1; -diagonal values Pearson correlations [-1, 1].

title: Functional connectivity matrix (200 parcels) — fc
text: Derived ts.

title: Leading eigenvector decomposition (LEiDA) — get_leida
text: Extract leading eigenvector series instantaneous phase-locking matrices. sign eigenvector normalised sum non-positive, following LEiDA convention.

title: Leading eigenvector decomposition (LEiDA) — get_leida
code: get_leida(sync_conn)

title: Leading eigenvector decomposition (LEiDA) — get_leida
text: sync_conn Numeric array [N, N, Tmax]. Instantaneous phase-locking matrices, returned dyn_phase_lock().

title: Leading eigenvector decomposition (LEiDA) — get_leida
text: Numeric matrix [Tmax × N]. Leading eigenvectors, one per timepoint.

title: Leading eigenvector decomposition (LEiDA) — get_leida
text: Cabral, J. et al. (2017). Cognitive performance healthy older adults relates spontaneous switching states functional connectivity rest. Scientific Reports, 7(1), 5135. doi:10.1038/s41598-017-05425-7 Lord, L.-D. et al. (2019). Dynamical exploration repertoire brain networks rest modulated psilocybin. NeuroImage, 199, 127–142. doi:10.1016/j.neuroimage.2019.05.060

title: Hilbert transform phase extraction — hilbert_phases
text: Compute instantaneous phase time series parcels/voxels via analytic signal (Hilbert transform). parcel timeseries demeaned transformation.

title: Hilbert transform phase extraction — hilbert_phases
code: hilbert_phases(timeseries)

title: Hilbert transform phase extraction — hilbert_phases
text: timeseries Numeric matrix [N × Tmax]. BOLD signal N parcels rows Tmax timepoints columns.

title: Hilbert transform phase extraction — hilbert_phases
text: Numeric matrix [N × Tmax]. Instantaneous phases radians.

title: Hilbert transform phase extraction — hilbert_phases
text: Cabral, J. et al. (2017). Cognitive performance healthy older adults relates spontaneous switching states functional connectivity rest. Scientific Reports, 7(1), 5135. doi:10.1038/s41598-017-05425-7

title: Hilbert transform phase extraction — hilbert_phases
code: set.seed(1) ts <- matrix(rnorm(10 * 200), nrow = 10, ncol = 200) phases <- hilbert_phases(ts) dim(phases)  # 10 x 200 #> [1]  10 200

what: Kuramoto order parameter and metastability — kuramoto
title: Kuramoto order parameter and metastability — kuramoto
text: Compute global Kuramoto order parameter time series, metastability index (standard deviation order parameter), Shannon entropy synchrony parcel-level instantaneous phase time series. first last 10 timepoints discarded (matching dyn_phase_lock()).

title: Kuramoto order parameter and metastability — kuramoto
code: kuramoto(phases, base = 2, n_bits = 8L)

title: Kuramoto order parameter and metastability — kuramoto
text: phases Numeric matrix [N × Tmax]. Instantaneous phases radians, returned hilbert_phases(). base Numeric. Logarithm base Shannon entropy. Default 2. n_bits Integer NULL. Bit depth discretising synchrony series entropy estimation. Default 8.

title: Kuramoto order parameter and metastability — kuramoto
text: list : metastability Numeric scalar. Standard deviation Kuramoto order parameter. synchrony Numeric vector [Tmax-20]. Kuramoto order parameter (trimmed) timepoint. entropy Numeric scalar. Shannon entropy synchrony series.

title: Kuramoto order parameter and metastability — kuramoto
code: set.seed(1) ts <- matrix(rnorm(10 * 200), nrow = 10, ncol = 200) phases <- hilbert_phases(ts) res <- kuramoto(phases) res$metastability #> [1] 0.1427287

title: Shannon entropy — shannon_entropy
text: Estimate Shannon entropy numeric vector, optional bit-depth discretisation (used internally kuramoto()).

title: Shannon entropy — shannon_entropy
code: shannon_entropy(x, base = 2, n_bits = NULL)

title: Shannon entropy — shannon_entropy
text: x Numeric vector. base Numeric. Logarithm base. Default 2 (entropy bits). n_bits Integer NULL. supplied, values scaled \(2^{n\_bits}\) rounded computing entropy. Default NULL.

title: Shannon entropy — shannon_entropy
text: Numeric scalar. Shannon entropy.

title: Shannon entropy — shannon_entropy
code: x <- sample(1:4, 100, replace = TRUE) shannon_entropy(x) #> [1] 1.984508

title: BOLD fMRI timeseries (200 parcels, 600 timepoints) — ts
text: resting-state BOLD fMRI timeseries edge-ts repository, parcellated 200 regions interest. Used dynR vignettes examples demonstrate dynamic FC methods realistic data.

title: BOLD fMRI timeseries (200 parcels, 600 timepoints) — ts
code: ts

title: BOLD fMRI timeseries (200 parcels, 600 timepoints) — ts
text: numeric matrix 200 rows (parcels) 600 columns (timepoints).

title: BOLD fMRI timeseries (200 parcels, 600 timepoints) — ts
text: https://github.com/brain-networks/edge-ts
